mission.title: Install and Configure Kyverno (Policy Engine)
mission.description: Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine that allows users to manage security, compliance, and governance through policy-as-code. It is particularly useful for teams looking to enforce policies consistently across their Kubernetes clusters. Key trade-offs include the need for a learning curve to understand policy definitions and potential performance impacts when enforcing complex policies.

steps.title: Add the Kyverno Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Kyverno Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno

helm repo update

```

> This adds the Kyverno repository for Helm.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install Kyverno with Helm
steps.description:
This command installs Kyverno into the kube-system namespace using the specified version.

```bash

helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kube-system --create-namespace --version 1.17.1

```

> Installs Kyverno with the specified version.

steps.title: Verify Kyverno installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Kyverno pods are running correctly in the kube-system namespace.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=kyverno

```

> You should see the Kyverno pods in a Running state.

steps.title: Create an example policy
steps.description:
This command creates a sample policy to demonstrate Kyverno's functionality.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

```

> This creates a policy that requires the 'app' label on Pods.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

steps.title: Verify the policy is enforced
steps.description:
This command checks if the policy is correctly enforcing the required labels on Pods.

```bash

kubectl run test-pod --image=nginx --restart=Never

kubectl get events

```

> This will fail if the 'app' label is not provided.
> Check for events related to policy enforcement.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Kyverno
uninstall.description:
This step removes Kyverno from your cluster. Be aware that any policies created will also be deleted, which may lead to data loss if they were enforcing critical configurations.

```bash

helm uninstall kyverno --namespace kube-system

kubectl delete namespace kube-system --ignore-not-found

```

> This command uninstalls the Kyverno release.
> This removes the namespace if it is no longer needed.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Kyverno
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your policies. This step will upgrade Kyverno to the latest version while ensuring the existing policies remain intact.

```bash

kubectl get clusterpolicies -o yaml > clusterpolicies-backup.yaml

helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kube-system --version 1.17.1

kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=kyverno

```

> Backup existing policies.
> Upgrade to the specified version.
> Verify that the Kyverno pods are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'validation failed'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that a resource does not comply with the defined policies.

**Fix:**
Check the policy definitions and ensure that the resources being created comply with the policies.

troubleshooting.title: Kyverno pods are in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to misconfiguration in the policies or insufficient resources.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Kyverno pods using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kube-system' and adjust the policies or resource limits accordingly.

troubleshooting.title: Policy not enforcing as expected
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The policy might not be applied correctly or the validationFailureAction is set incorrectly.

**Fix:**
Verify the policy is applied using 'kubectl get clusterpolicies' and ensure the validationFailureAction is set to 'enforce'.

troubleshooting.title: Unable to create resources due to policy violation
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The resource being created does not meet the policy requirements.

**Fix:**
Review the policy requirements and ensure that the resource definition complies with them.

versionNotes.changes: Improved performance for policy validation, added support for new Kubernetes features.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Kyverno pods running in the kube-system namespace and the example policy should be enforced without errors.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF

kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-labels
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce
  rules:
    - name: check-labels
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the specified resources and the necessary tools installed.
